perturbation.original_text: Client shall conduct all Acceptance Tests in good faith and shall not delay any acceptance of any Service or Deliverable without reasonable justification. The evaluation of any Service or Deliverable for any Acceptance Test will be based on material compliance with applicable Specifications and Client shall not arbitrarily withhold acceptance of any Milestone Deliverable or Subject Program.
perturbation.changed_text: Client shall endeavor to conduct Acceptance Tests and shall consider reasonable justifications for any delay in the acceptance of any Service or Deliverable. The evaluation of any Service or Deliverable for any Acceptance Test will be based on perceived compliance with applicable Specifications, and Client shall strive not to arbitrarily withhold acceptance of any Milestone Deliverable or Subject Program.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall conduct' with 'shall endeavor to conduct,' 'shall not delay' with 'shall consider reasonable justifications,' 'material compliance' with 'perceived compliance,' and 'shall not arbitrarily withhold acceptance' with 'shall strive not to arbitrarily withhold acceptance' weakens the obligation, making it subjective. The term 'perceived compliance' is vague and shifts the burden of proof. "endeavor", "consider", and "strive" do not mandate action.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Washington law implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By weakening the language regarding acceptance tests, the modification creates ambiguity as to whether the client is genuinely evaluating the software in good faith based on the specifications, or acting according to a more subjective interpretation. 'Endeavor', 'consider', 'perceived compliance,' and 'strive' give the client significant leeway to avoid acceptance for reasons not strictly related to the specifications, potentially violating the implied duty of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 62A.1-304: Obligation of good faith. Print RCWs > Title 62A > Chapter 62A.1 > Section 62A.1-304 62A.1-303 << 62A.1-304 >> 62A.1-305 PDF RCW 62A.1-304 Obligation of good faith. Every contract or duty within this title imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. [ 2012 c 214 s 118 .] NOTES: Application — Savings — 2012 c 214: See notes following RCW 62A.1-101 .
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the client's obligation to conduct acceptance tests in good faith, potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing required in contracts. The original text mandates 'shall conduct' and 'shall not delay,' while the changed text uses weaker, less binding language such as 'shall endeavor to conduct' and 'shall consider reasonable justifications.' Furthermore, the shift from 'material compliance' to 'perceived compliance' introduces subjectivity. RCW 62A.1-304 states that 'Every contract or duty within this title imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The altered language allows for the client to avoid acceptance for reasons not directly tied to specifications, contradicting the obligation of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: Developer shall use its best efforts to make any necessary corrections, modifications, bug-fixes, or other changes promptly to complete the Services and Deliverables by the Final Delivery Date.
perturbation.changed_text: Developer shall attempt to make corrections, modifications, bug-fixes, or other changes to complete the Services and Deliverables by the Final Delivery Date.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'best efforts' with 'attempt' significantly reduces the developer's obligation to correct errors and complete the project on time. 'Best efforts' implies a high level of diligence and commitment, while 'attempt' only suggests a try without any guarantee of success.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to Perform
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Washington law, a breach of contract occurs when a party fails to perform a duty imposed by the contract. By reducing the developer's commitment from 'best efforts' to merely 'attempting,' the modified text makes it easier for the developer to avoid liability for failing to complete the project on time and according to specifications. A judge/jury could find it difficult to find a breach for failure to complete a project when only an 'attempt' was promised. This significantly undermines the enforceability of the delivery schedule and acceptance testing clauses, potentially leading to a breach of contract claim. Under Washington law, a duty to perform is defined using objective standards. Changing this to 'attempt' introduces ambiguity to an otherwise enforceable obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Washington State Courts - Error Washington Courts Home Search | Site Map | eService Center Washington State Courts You have encountered an Error. There was either a problem on the website, or you have requested a page that does not exist or is no longer available on our web site. If you are searching for a page which used to be available, or if you have discovered a link that is broken, please let us know . You may be able to find an alternate page by Searching the Site , or by reviewing the Site Map . Courts | Organizations | News | Opinions | Rules | Forms | Directory | Library
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires the developer to use 'best efforts,' which implies a high level of diligence. The changed text replaces this with 'attempt,' significantly weakening the developer's obligation. The Law Explanation states that this change 'makes it easier for the developer to avoid liability' and 'introduces ambiguity to an otherwise enforceable obligation.' Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is an error message, it highlights the importance of accessible and functional legal information, implying a standard of clarity and enforceability that the modified text undermines. The perturbation shifts a concrete obligation to a discretionary act, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Client will pay Fees or all Services and Deliverables provided by Developer to Client up to and including the ate of termination; and (c) in compliance with Section 6.1 of this Agreement, Developer will transfer all Works to Client as of the date of termination.
perturbation.changed_text: Client will settle Fees for all Services and Deliverables provided by Developer to Client up to the date of termination; and (c) Developer will endeavor to transfer Works to Client as of the date of termination, resources permitting.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'pay Fees' to 'settle Fees' introduces ambiguity regarding the exact amount to be paid, potentially leading to disputes over outstanding balances. The addition of 'resources permitting' to the developer's obligation to transfer works makes the obligation conditional and uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to Pay and Transfer Ownership
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 62A.2-301 states that the obligation of the seller (Developer, in this case with respect to intellectual property) is to transfer and deliver, and that of the buyer (Client) is to accept and pay in accordance with the contract. By stating 'settle Fees' instead of 'pay Fees', the contract is ambiguous as to how much is owed, and therefore contradicts the requirement for a buyer to pay according to the contract. By introducing resource constraints ('resources permitting'), the modification introduces ambiguity which undermines the developer's obligation to transfer ownership. It might be difficult to litigate, should the developer refuse to do so, claiming they did not have enough resources.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 62A.2-301: General obligations of parties. Print RCWs > Title 62A > Chapter 62A.2 > Section 62A.2-301 62A.2-210 << 62A.2-301 >> 62A.2-302 PDF RCW 62A.2-301 General obligations of parties. The obligation of the seller is to transfer and deliver and that of the buyer is to accept and pay in accordance with the contract. [1965 ex.s. c 157 s 2-301. Cf. former RCW sections: (i) RCW 63.04.120 ; 1925 ex.s. c 142 s 11 ; RRS s 5836-11. (ii) RCW 63.04.420 ; 1925 ex.s. c 142 s 41 ; RRS s 5836-41.]
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity and conditionality that undermines the original obligation. Specifically, changing 'pay Fees' to 'settle Fees' introduces ambiguity regarding the exact amount owed, contradicting the buyer's obligation to 'accept and pay in accordance with the contract,' as stated in RCW 62A.2-301. Furthermore, adding 'resources permitting' to the developer's obligation to transfer works makes this obligation conditional, also contradicting the seller's obligation to 'transfer and deliver' according to the same RCW.
